-~ LIMERICK.

LIMERICE is a city, municipal and parliamentary
borough, the seat of an episcupal see, and & considerable
commercial port and railway centre, 1zo miles soumth-
west from Dublin, 77 north-west from Waterford, 61
north from /Cork, 51 north-eass from Tralee, 53 east
from XKilrush and 75 east from Dunmore Head, an
extreme apex of Clare county, protruding into the
Atlantic Qcean. This anciens city, delightfully situated
on the eastern bank of the Shannon, is supposed to
have been founded by Yuoras, in the year 155, while,
by some writers, it is said to have been the “ Regia” of
Ptolemy. It was called * Rosse-de-Nailleagh™ in the
Annals of Multifernan, and is belisved to have been the
_place described under the name of “ Luimneach,” which
name is supposed to have been modified to its present
.designation. St. Patrick is said to have visited it about
the middle of the sth centery; but the first authentic
records of Limerick notice it as a Danish settlement;
by that people it was first plundered in 81a. The
Danes of Limerick did not embrace Christianity antil
the r1th century, and in the following they elected their
Girst bishop. Before this period, however, it was a
place of commerce, and was much frequented by
foreign merchants. The English took possession of
Limerick in the year r1y4; and as a proof of the early
importance of the city, in the gth year of the reign of
EKing Richard I. (r1g7), he granted a charter to the
citizens to elect a mayor, which honour was not obtained
-by the citizens of London till 1o years after that period;
nor had Dublin and Cork a mayor till the 13th centurs.
The city was originally walled, and deemed. the strongest
fortress in this kingdom, having the advantage of not
‘being commanded by adjacent heights, 2 it has
-gustained some memorable sieges. In the vear 10go
K.Eng William III. brought his forces against it, but
withdrew them without accomplishing its reduction; in
the [ollowing year it was again invested by General
Ginckel, who, after an obstinate resistance, compelled
the garriscn to surrender. on honourable terms of
capitulation.  During the siege the Irish fought
gallantly, and repulsed the English several times, but
on z3rd Jept. 1691, an amicable intercourse was opened
between che two econtending parties.  The treaty was
signed (Oct. 3rd, Sgr) on the stone which is now
erected on a pedestal at the western end of Thomond
bridge. William IIL. broke faith after ratifying the
agreement, totally disregarding if, hence Limerick has
been called the “City of the Violated Treaty.” In 1703
an Act was passed excluding Boman Catholic strangers
from-residing in the citv, end requiring from the per-
manent inhabitants of that persuasion sureties for their
allegiance under pain of expulsion; but in the year 1724
these illiberal and arbitrary restrictions wers removed.
In.1760 Limerick was declared to be no longer a fortress,
and the dismantling of its walls and other defences was
then commenced and proceeded with, as the extension
of the various improvements rendered the removal of
old buildings necessary. On the bresking ont of the
American war in 181z, the lovalty of this city and
district was manifested by the alacrity with which the
wolunteer corps were embodied under the names of the
#“Limerick Union,” the ¢ Loval Limerick Volanteers,”
and the “ Limerick Veolonteers.” After the termination
of the contest with America, the improvements in and
extension of the city were renewed with energy, un-
retarded by the oceasional alarms of insurrection, which
distracted some "of the neighbouring districts. In 1803
a design was formed by these engaged in Emmett's con-
spiracy to take the city by surprise, and with so much
‘seerecy was the plan conduacted that it was unknown to
the military commandant until the evening preceding
the day of intended attack, when measures were taken
4o frustrate the design. Portions of the old wall are
still in existence, the most interesting remains lying
between Lock gquay and St John's Hospital. The
gmnnd near the latter building formed the Black
* Battery, between which and 3t. John's gate, a breach was

¢ -1

' effected by the army of William IT1.

tons and cleared 11 vessels, with 8,318 tons.

The wall here is
36 feet thick., This city is now composed of three parts,
named the Irish Town, English Town {the latter on an
island formed by the river Shannon), and Newtown-
Pery, which latter may with propriety be termed the
court end, as in this guarter of the town the sireets ara
spacious, uniform and elegant, intersecting each other
at right angles, and the houses of modern architecturs.
This portion of the city takes its name from, and was
erected by the exertions of the Right Hon. Edmund
Sexton Pery, Speaker of the Irish House of Commons,
created in 1785 Viscount Pery of Newtown-Pery, and who
died in 1806, when the title expired. It is the fashion-
able as well as favourite residence of the opulent.

Lord Emly of Tervoe, Limerick, is lord of the manor.

Limerick is an important junction on the Greal
Southern and Western railway, the main line from
Waterford entering the city from the south-east and
continuing to the northwards, the Foynes and Tralee
branch ranning in a south-westerly direction, while to
the north-east is a branch to Killaloe. By a junction
on the main line at Enanis communication is made with
the West Clare railway, The city has alse, via Charle-
ville junction, direct communication with the city of
Cork.

The river Shannon flows from Lough Derg, and after
forming the cataract of Doonas passes through the city
of Limerick to the estuary, which is nearly o miles in
length, snd receives the Fergus about 12 miles below
the city. The river is crossed by two principal snd
several minor bridges; the Wellesley or Sarsfield bridge,
called after Richard, 1st and only Marquess of Wellesley
E.G., KE.P. and sometime Lord Lieutenant of Ireland,
was commenced in 1821, and opened to the public in
1833, at a cost of about fBo,000; it consists of five
large elliptical arches, and of & swivel drawbridge on
the south side; at the eastern end is a statue of Vis-
count FitzGibbon (8th Hussars), only son of 3rd and
last Earl of Clare, killed at Balaclava, during the
Crimean war, Oct. 25th, 1854. Thomond bridge, which
connects King's Isiand or English Town with the
suburl lying in co. Clare, was rebuilt in 1830, and is
500 feet in length; at its western end, on a moulded
pedestal raised on four steps, is the famous Treaty
Stone, on which, on 3 Oct. g1, a treaty was signed,
by which the freedom of the Catholic religion was
secured and various forms of relief granted, but the
treaty was annulled by the Irish Parliament in 1hg3.
On the Abbey river, an arm of the Shannon sarround-
ing King’s Island, is Mathew bridge (named after the
celebrated apostle of temperance, Father Mathew), Balls
bridge and Park bridge.

The port and harbour of Limerick are at the head of
the estuary of the Shannon, and have s floating dock,
constructed at an expense of fso.000, and a griwing
dock, 438 feet in length, constructed at a cost o nearly
{lm..ono. The quay walls afford extensive facilities for
the loading and discharging of vessels of light tonnage.
The Limerick Harbour Commissioners have the control
and regulation of the harbour, and are entitled by
their Act to levy tolls on all shipping freguenting the
port, aa well as on all imports and exports, snd from
these and other sources they realise a handsome vearly
revenue. The imports consist principally of timber,
coal, grain, salt, sugar &c. The value of the principal
articles imported in 1904 was: wheat, [485,034; maize,
Eng.:s:; sugar, £132,783; total value of all imports,

935,074. The shipping that entered and cleared in
the port of Li|:|£':.nt'.~~1~-1".::lf:l in 1904 was as follows:—Entered
with cargoes and in ballast, British Possessions, 13
sailing asnd steam vessels; tonnage, 4,537; cleared, 8
vessels: toonage, 7,375. Foreign, 5t vessels, 53,471
In the
general coasting trade g6 sailing and steam vessels
entered with cargoes and in ballast of 11,503 tons, and
133 cleared of 23,08g tonnage. In the intercourse be-
tween Great Britain and Ireland, 438 vessels entered of

100,003 tons, and 440 cleared of 143,351 tonnage. The |
number of ships registered as belnn{lng to this port on
December 31, 1904, under the * Merchant bhlgpln%‘ Aect,
was a3, with a tonnage of 1,741. The number of boats
engaged in fisheries. and registered under part IV, of
the Merchant Shipping Act,” was at the same date
160, Tequiring 437 mea and boys to work them. Fish-
ing boats and their implements are distingunished by |
the letter #L.” .

About a mile distant from the city and below |
Sarsfield bridge is the Pool, where vessels anchor, and |
from which a most delightful view is afforded. On one
side is the New Town, with its guays Land the Bishcp's
palace, with its gardens close to the river, and on the
other side a number of villas belonging to the wealthy
citizens; from this point a Tull view of the city is
obtained, the whole forming an interesting panoramic
picture. The domaestic trade of Limerick is very ex-
tensive. The linen and woollen manuofacture, once a
staple branch of trade, disappeared scme yesrs ago, a_mld
the manunfactare of lace and gloves, for which Lamerica
was at one time famous, has been succeeded by other
industries, but lace making is still pursued at the
Convent, in Clare street. Chief amongst these is bacon
curing, which is largely carried on, and the factories
erected for the purpose are extensive and well equipped.
A considerable export trade is also carried on in buiter
and eggs, and butterine is manufactured here. The army
clothing factory, erected by Sir Peter Tait, who died in
18go, furnishes employment to npwards of 1,000 hands.
Other industries of Limerick comprise the manufacture
of condensed milk, flonr, tobacco &c.; there are
extensive tan yards and a distillery.

The city is lighted with gas from works purchased by
the Corporation in 1879, from the local gas company, and
the supply is now controlled by a mixed representative
committee of five members. The water works are slso
the property of the Corporation. ;

The Corporation of Lumerick existed both by prescrip-
tion and charter. The city received privileges, fran-
chises and charters from the early date of 1193, when
John Stafford was made its first provost; the frst
mayor being Adam Servant, in n%a. The provisions in
the charter conferred by James II, remained in force
until the Act of the 4th of George 1V. (1823-4). called
the “Limerick Regulation Act,” partially remcdelled
the powers of the Corporation. The “ Municipal Beform
Act”. became law in Limerick g9 Nov. 1841. The
present corporate bedy, :trled the “ Mayor, Aldermen
and Burgesses of Limerick,” consists of eight aldermen
and 32 councillors, elected from out of the eight
wards into which the city is divided. The mayor holds
a court of conseience weekly, for adjndicating claims of
36s. 10d. or under. The assizes for the county and
county of the city are held twice a year by the judges
of the High Court of Justice in Ireland; the court of
quarter sessions is held before the county court judge
and chairman of gquarter sessions, the mayor and other
justices; petty sessions once a week, before the mayer
and some of the civic magistrates, and a police court
daily. The county court judge fox the eounty sits four
times a vear for the trial of civil bill cases. The city
returned two members to the Irish Parliament up to
the date of the Union in 1800, and subsequently the
same number to the Imperial Parliament, until by the
% Redistribution of Seats Act, 188:,” the number was
reduced to one. The parliamentary borough is, how-
ever, more extensive than the municipal.

The Town Hall, in Butland street, contains the couneil
chamber of the City Corporation and the Mayor's,
Town Clerk's and Treasurer's offices. ;

z Tlaf. Court House, near Merchants' quay, was erected
in 1810. -

The muonicipal insignia include four maces, a state
sword, and a wmayoral chain of very remarkable
character, The maces are similar in size, design and
workmanship: they are all 34 inches long, and their
shafts are chased with spiral foliage; each has two
knops and a graceful base knop, on which are the
nemes of the mavor and sheriffs for 17359; at the tap
of each shaft, instead of the usual scrolled brackets,
there are four rampant lions, facing outwards, each
resting one leg on the upper knop, and their heads
touching the heads of the maces; these are of the
usual ball cup shape, and are encircled with a garland
of foliage and flowers, partly disposed in four pendants
forming arches, under which appear the ecity srms—
a double-towered gateway—a rose and thistle on one
stem, 3 harp and a fleur-de-lis; above the foliage is a
narrow beaded circlet, and on the flat top are the roval
arms of George II. The sword, given by Queen Eliza-
beth in 1575, is 44 inches long; the guard of iron-gilt is
:_ﬂ:'_ inches across, and the ends curve up towards the

LIMERICK,

[KELLY’S

blade; the grip is eight imches long and is covered
with green velvet, secured with pilt wire; the pommel
is a round fiat disc, attached by the edge. The mayor's
chain consists of so gold roundels or fat rings, vary-
ing in size; from 182z up to 1241 these were linked into
each other, but after that date they were joined together
in pairs by double links; on each ring or roundel i=
engraved the name of a mavor and his vear of office.

%he see of Limerick in the provinece of Dublin is stated
to have been founded in the 6th century by St. Munchin,
but the bishops can only be definitely traced from 1140,
when Gillebert was appointed; in 1657 the united dio-
cese of Ardfert and Aghadoe was anpexed to it. The
cathedral church of St. Mary, originally built between
t142 and 1180, and added to in the 13th century, was
considerably altered in the rsth and 15th centuries, tut
still retains its cruciform plan, and consists of choir.
transepts, clerestoried nave of three bays with wide
aisles, south porch and a lofty embattled western tower
with tall sguare pinnacles at the angles, finished with
stepped battlements, the total height being 120 feet:
the tower contains a peal of § bells, originally cast in
1573, but some were recast in 1829 and 1839. The
building was repaired in 1739, at a cost of f1,327, when
the choir was enlarged and its arches glazed and fittings
of classic character introduced. In 186c the cathedral
was restored, the choir new-roofed, a new east window
inserted and the floors re-laid, under the direction of
Mr. Slater, architect. of London, and in 1869 further
repairs and alterations were effected: the total lengih
of the fabric is about 170, and the width through the
transepts oo feet. The choir, continued beyond the
transepts, contains zo stalls on each side, several of
which, with their misereres, date from the 15th cen-
tury: canopies were added to the stalls at the restora-
tion of 1850: the bishop's throme, erected in 283z, from
designs by Mr. James Paine, stands at the east end of
the range of stalls on the south side, and beyond it is a
canopied seat occupied officially by the Earl of Limerick
as Prior of St. Mary's: the north tramsept serves as an
organ chamber, the south transept has five graduaced
lancat lights within a stepped gable, the aisles are
embattled and sub-divided, so as to form chapels, ome
of which, on the south side, has some good stained
glass, and contains the tombs of the Earls of Limerick =
the sonth aisle projects beyond the transept 2nd hos
some Perpendicular windows: in the south transept is a
fine monument to the Gallwey family, dated 1416, and
on the north side of the sacrarium is a large altar
tomb, with colomns supporting a canopy, and recum-
bent effigies, disposed in two tiers, to Donogh O'Brien,
4th Earl of Thomond, d. 5 Sept. 1624, and Elizabeth
g’itzgarﬂdj, his wife, dan. of Gerald, Earl of Kildare,

12 Jan. 1617: among the numerous other memoriads
are those of Donat O'Brien, bishop 1207-17, and
Cornelius 0'Dea, bishop 1400-26. The registers, number-
ing nine volumes, date from 1726, and are kept hepe
under a retention order.

-Opposite the cathedral is the statue of the celebrated
General Patrick Sarsfield, Earl of Lucan, who compelled
William III. to sign the treaty of Limerick; the figure,
which stands on a high pedestal, represents him sword
in hand, leading on his troops.

The Bishop's Palace iz in Henry street, and the
grounds stretch down to O'Neill’s quay, on the east
bank of the river. :

St. Munchin’s church, King’s Island. founded in the
7th century, and rebuilt in 1827 at a cost of L1460, i=
a plain stroctare, and has an embattled tower with
pinnacles; this church was the burial place of many
ancient Limerick families, and will zseat 300 persons.
Trinity church, Catherine street, was opened in 1834
and will seat 8c0. St. John the Baptist's, in St. Johni=
square, affords 4oo sittings, and St. Michael's, in Perv
squarae, 750.

The Catholic diocese of Limeriek includes Limerick
and & small portion of Clare. 5t. Jobn's, the Catholic
cathedral, erscted in 1836-00, at a cost of about fao,000,
is an edifice in the Gothic stvle of the 13th century,
and consists of chancel, nave, aisles and transepts, and &
tower with spire oo feet in height. Tha bishop resides
at Corbally. St. Mary's Catholic church, Athlunkard
street, was erected in 1745. St. Michael’s Catholic
charch, originally standing in Mardyke, was destroyed
in the siege of t6g:, but rebuilt in Denmark street, and
entirely rebuilt on this site and remodelled in 188z
St. Joseph's church, in Quinlan street, which is attached
to St. Michael's, was completed in 1904. The church
of the Dominican convent, erected in 18135, and re-
decorated in 1go3z, is & bunilding in the Gothic style.
The Bedemptorist monastery (Mount St. Alphonsus),
was founded in 1853, and the present building erected
t55-58; the church, built in 1358-62, from deszigns By

4

DIRECTORY. ]

Mr. Philip C. Hardwick, is an edifice in the First-Pointed
style of the 1ath or :3th century, and consists of apsidal
chancel, double transepts with side chapels, nave with
aisles and a tower with spire, added during 1876-78,
and containing a peal of bells; the total cost of monas-
tery and church, including the site, was upwards of
£23,000. The Aungustinian church, in George street,
once a theatre, was consecrated in 1823, and is elegantly
embellished and a fine scriptural painting adorns the
altar.

The Franciscan church, in Henry street, is a fine
hu.ildin%} with massive colamus at the main entrance.

The Wesleyan chapel, George street, was erected in
1810, There is a Baptist chapel on the Military road; a
Presbyterian church, in Lower Mallow street, built in
1502; a Gospel Hall, in Tpper Mallow street, and a
meeting house, in Upper Cecil street, for the Society of
Friends.

The Limerick Athenmum, Upper Cecil street, was
erected chiefly with the intention of developing litera-
ture, science and art, and has a lecture hall capable of
accommodating nearly 6zo persons; the classes of the
Municipal School of Science and Art are held in this
building. The “ Free Library Act ™ was adopted in 1291,
and a large private house in Lower Glentworth street
was fitted up for the purpose of a free public library
and reading room, at a cost of {6oo, and opened in
1893; a new building, the gift of Andrew Carnegie ezq.
LL.D. of Skibo Castle, N.B. is now (1go3) in course of
erection in Pery street.

The Limerick Institution, George street, is supported
chiefly by professional men and merchants, and possesses
a reading room and a splendid library.

. The Chamber of Commerce, in George street, formed
in the vear 1808, and incorporated by royal charter in
1813, “for the promotion and protection of the trade of
Limerick,” contains news rooms and a library, s board
room for the use of the Harbour Commissioners and
Market Trustees, and an office for the secretary and
collecter of the harbour; this institution comprises
among its members the principal merchants of the ecity.

The Custorn House is a fine building of hewn stone,
embellished in front with fluted pilasters and capitals,
and on each side is a piazza. The amount of customs
revenue collected in 1904 was L143,210.

There is a theatre in Henry street.

. The County Infirmary, in Mulgrave street, was opened
in 1811, 8t & cost of {f,m.

Barrinagmn’a Hospital, at George's quay, was founded
by Bir Joseph Barrington bart. of Limerick, and his |
sons in 1829; it cost [Lro,000, and was opened for
patients in 1831, -

The City Dispensary is in Lower Gerald Griffin streat,
and serves four districts,

The District Lunatic Asylum, in Mulgrave street, was
erected in 1825 at a cost of £30,000.

St. John's Fever and General Hospital is in St. John's
gate; the Lying-in Hospital in Bedford row, and the
Asylum for Blind Females in Catherine street, and
there are several orphanages and a reformatory, con-
ducted by the Catholic sisterhoods.

The Nash Home, Bichmond terrace, was founded in |

r:gf, by a bequest of Mr. John Nash, of Limerick, for
ladies in redoced circumstances, who must be natives cf
eo. Limerick. The Villiers Almshouses, The Island,
were founded snd endowed by Mrs. Villiers, of Limerick,
for widows. The Corporation Almshouses, in Nicholas
street, and Dr. HalP's Almshouses, in the same, are
both for widows. .

The Bank of Ireland Limited, the Provincial Bank of
Ireland. the National Bank Limited, the Munster and
Leinster Bank Limited, and the Ulster Bank Limited
have each a branch here,

There are two good hotels, and some smaller ones.

Fmn_'  newspapers are published—the “ Limerick
Chmnmia,”_un Tuesday, Thursday and Saturday; the
* Munster News,” on Monday, Wednesday and Saturday ;
the “Limerick Leader,” Monday, Wednesday and Fri-
day, and the ¥ Limerick Echo,” Tuesday and Friday.

There are now four schools in Limerick in connection
with the Christian Brothers, where orphan bovs are
tanght different trades. Limerick District Modsl
school, Military road, was built in 1853, and is under
t!m management of the Commissioners of National
Education. Leamy's Free schools, Hartstonge street,
were founded in 1814, at a cost, including site, of

3:040, from a bequest by William Leamy esq. who leit

13,300 for the education of the children of the pocr in
reland, especially those in the neighboarkood of
Limerick city; the schools will hold 6oo children :
average attendance, 4oo. '

LIMERICK, 89

| acres; the population in zgor was:—Abington {part of),

H.M. Prison, in Mulgrave street, for males only,
consists of & large central block, containing the
governor's apartments, a dispensary and a chapel; there
are four blocks of buildings assigned to prisoners, and
the airing vard is overlooked from the centre building.
H.M. Prison for females is in Merchants' guay.

There arz barracks for cavalrr, artillery and infantry,
all commodions and in airy situations. The city forms
part of the Cork District of the Irish Military Command.
The Limerick City Royal Garrison Artillery (Militis)

{ and the sth Battalion Roval Munster Fusiliers (Militia)
have also separate barracks. The South of Ireland Im-
perial Yeomanry have also head qunarters in the city.

Limerick is the bead quarters of the counoty con-
stabulary.

The market places are very extensive and are situated
in different parts of the city, the principsl being the
New Market, near Garrvowen, which are not now sur-
passed for extent, fenerul adaptability and style of con-
struction in Ireland. The markers are held on Wednes-
day and Saturday, and are abundantly supplied with
all the necessaries of life at remarkably reasonable
prices, Fairs :—Tuesday in Easter week, July 4th,
Augnst 4th and December 1zth. The great Munster
fairs are held the l2st Thursday and Friday in
Jonuary, April, July and October, on Thursday, horses
and pigs, and on Friday cattle and sheep., and at the
monthly fairs, pigs first Tuesdar. caitle frst Thursday.

There is a privilege annexed to the August fair—
namely, that no person can be arrested within the
precincts of the liberties, on any process issuing out of
the tholsel court of Limerick, for the space of 15 davs.

The district around Limerick is agricultural. The
land is flat and werv fertile, but there is an abundance
of beantiful scznery in the vicinity of the Clare and
Killaloe hills, which are not far distant. The soil is
limestone and sandy. A large salmon and eel weir at
Corbally, about a mile from Limerick, with a several
fishery extending for 6 miles westward, is owned by
the Shannon Fishing Company Limited. ‘

People’s Park.—The late Earl of Limerick K.P. pre-
sented to the Corporation, in 1875, land of about 1o
acres for a park, in memory of the Iate Richard Bussell
esq. of Limerick; it has been laid out, and contains a
monument erected to the Right Hon. Thomas Spring
Rice, 15t Lord Monteagle.

New Park.—A portion of the grounds adjoining the
Custom House has been granted by the Board of
Works to the Corporation, at a nominal rent, to be
used as & park or recreation ground for the citizens.

The Green Park Racecourse, of 149 acres, has been
purchased by the Limerick Raczcourse Co. Limited,
It is situated about a mile south-west of the city, and
has two spacions stands erected, capable of accommo-
dating 3,000 people. There is a directorate of manage-
ment of 16 members; J. O'G. Delmege esq. J.P., D.L.
chairman,

ez LIMERICK.

LIMERICK is a city, municipal and parliamentary
borough, the seat of an episcopal see, and a considerable
commercial port and railway centre, 120 miles sputh-
west from Dublin, 77 north-west from Waterford, 61
north from ;Cork, 1 north-eass from Tralee, 53 east
from XKilrash and 75 east from Dunmore Head, an
.extreme apex of Clare county, protruding into the
Atlantic Ocean. This anciens city, delightfully situated
on the easstern bank of the Shannon, is supposed to
have been founded by Yuorns, in the year 155, while,
by some writers, it is said to bave been the © ia " of
Ptolemy. It was called *Rosse-de-Nailleagh™ in the
. Annals of Multifernan, and is believed to have been the
_place described under the name of ~ Luimneach,” which
name is supposed to have been modified to its present
-designation. St, Patrick is said to have visited it about
the middle of the sth centurr; but the first authentic
records of Limerick notice it as a Danish settlement;
by that people it was first plundered in Bra. The
Danes of Limerick did not embrace Christianity until
the rrth centary, and in the following they elected their
Girst bishop. Before this period, however, it was a
lace of commerce, and was much f{requented by
reign merchants. The English took possession of
Limerick in the year r174; and as a proof of the early
importance of the city, in the oth vear of the reign of
Eing Richard I. (11g7), he granted a charter to the
citizens to elect a mayor, which honour was not obtained
-by the citizens of London till 1o years after that period;
nor had Dublin and Cork a mayor till the 13th century.
The city was originally walled, and deemed the strongest
fortress in this kingdom, having the advantage of not
being commanded by adjacent heights, and it haa
-gustained some memorable sieges. In the year 1690
King William III. brought his forces against it, but
withdrew them without accomplishing its reduction; in
the following year it was again invested by General
Ginckel, who, after an nobstinate resistance, compelled
the garriscn to surrender. on honourable terms of
<apitulation. During the siege the Irish fought
gallantly, and repulsed the English several times, but
on 23rd Jept. 1691, an amicable intercourse was opened
between he two contending parties. The treaty was
aigned (Oct. 3rd, rS91) on the stone which is now
erected on a pedestal at the western end of Thomond
bridge. William III. broke faith after ratilying the
agreement, totally disregarding it, hence Limerick has
been called the “City of the Violated Treaty.” In 1703
an Act was passed excluding Boman Catholic strangers
Imm‘reﬁding in the citv, snd requiring from the per-
manent inhabitants of that persuasion sureties for their
allegiance under pain of expulsion; but in the year 1724
these illiberal and arbitrary restrictions were removed.
In.1760 Limerick was declared to be no longer a fortress,
and the dismantling of its walls and other delfences was
then commenced and proceeded with, as the extension
of the various improvements rendered the removal of
old bmildings necessary. On the breaking out of the
Ameriean war in 1812, the lovalty of this city and
district was manifested by the alacrity with which the
yolunteer corps were embodied under the names of the
“Limerick Union,” the “Loyal Limerick Volunteers,”
and the * Limerick Volonteers.” After the termination
of the contest with America, the improvements in and
extension of the city were renewed with energy, un-
retarded by the occasional alarms of insurrection, which
distracted some "of the neighbouring districts. In 1803
2 design was formed by thess engaged in Emmett’s con-
gpiracy to take the city by surprise, and with so much
gecrecy was the plan conducted that it was unknown to
the military commandant until the evening preceding
‘the day of intended attack. when measures were taken
to frustrate the design. Portions of the old wall are
still in existence, the most interesting remains lying
betwesn Lock gquav and St. John's Hospital. The
ground mnear the latter building formed the Black
' Battery, between which and 3t John's gate, a breach was

effected by the army of William III. The wall here is
36 feet thick. This city is now composed of three parts,
named the Irish Town, English Town (the latter on an
island formed by the river Shannon), and Newtown-
Perv, which latter may with propriety be termed the
court end, as in this quarter of the town the sireets ara
spacious, uniform and elegant, intersecting each other
at right angles, and the houses of modern architecture.
This portion of the city takes its name from, and was
erected by the exertions of the Right Hon. Edmund
Sexton Pery, Speaker of the Irish House of Commons,
created in 1785 Viscount Pery of Newtown-Pery, and who
died in 1806, when the title expired. It is the fashion-
able as well as favourite residence of the opulent.

Lord Emly of Tervoe, Limerick, is lord of the manor.

Limerick is an important junction on the Great
Southern and Western railway, the main line from
Waterford entering the city from the south-east and
continuing to the northwards, the Foynes and Tralee
branch running in a south-westerly direction, while to
the north-east is a branch to Killalve. By a junction
on the main line at Ennis communiecation is made with
the West Clare railway. The city has also, via Charle-
Eillak junction, direct commuaication with the city of

OrxK.

The river Shannon flows from Lough Derg, and after
forming the cataract of Doonas passes through the city
of Limerick to the estuary, which is nearly 5o miles in
length, snd receives the Fergus about 12 miles below
the city. The river is crossed by two principal and
geveral minor bridees; the Wellesley or Sarafield bridge,
ealled after Richard, 15t and only Marquess of Wellesley
E.G., KE.P. and sometime Lord Lieutenant of Ireland,
was commenced in 1821, and opened to the public in
1833, at 3 cost of about LB8o,000; it consists of five
large elliptical arches, and of a swivel drawbridge on
the south side; at the eastern end is a statue of Vis-
count FitzGibbon (8th Hussars), only son of 3rd and
last Earl of Clare, killed at Balaclava, during the
Crimean war, Oct. 25th, 1833, Thomond bridge, which
connects King’s Island or English Town with the
subnrb lying in co. Clare, was rebuilt in 1839, and is
500 Peet in length; at its western end, on a3 moulded
pédestal raised on four steps, is the famous Treaty
Stone, on which, on 3 Oct. 1591, & treaty was signed,
by which the freedom of the Catholic religion was
secured and various forms of relief granted, but the
treaty was annulled by the Irish Parliament in 1595.
On the Abbey river, an arm of the Shannon surround-
ing King's Island, is Mathew bridge (named after the
celebrated apostle of temperance, Father Mathew), Balls
bridge and Park bridge.

The port and harbour of Limerick are at the head of
the estuary of the Shannon, and have a floating deck,
constructed at an expense of Ls0,000, and a ving
dock, 438 feet in length, constructed at a cost of nearly
{21,000, The quay walls afford extensive facilities for
the loading and discharging of vessels of light tonnage.
The Limerick Harbour Commissioners have the control
and regulation of the harbour, and are emtitled by
their Act to levy tolls on all shipping frequenting the
port, ss well as on all imports and exports, and from
these and other sources they realise a handsome yearly
revenue. The imports consist principally of timber,
coal, grain, salt, sugar &c. The value of the principal
articles imported in 1904 was: wheat, £485,034; maize,

About a mile distant from the city and below
Sarsfield bridge is the Pool, where vessels anchor, and
from which a most delightful view is afforded. On one
side is the New Town, with its quays and the Bishcp's
palace, with its gardens close to the river, and on the
other side a number of villas belonging to the wealthy
citizens; from this point a full view of the city is
obtained, the whole forming an interesting panoramic
picture. The domestic trade of Limerick is very ex-
tensive, The linen and woollen mannfacture, once 4
staple branch of trade, disappeared some years ago, a._n:i
the manufacture of lace and gloves, for which Limerics
was at one time famouns, has been succeeded by other
industries, but lace making is still pursued at the
Convent, in Clare street. Chief amongst these is bacon
curing, which is largely carried on, and the factories
erected for the purpose are extensive and well equipped.
A considerable export trade is also carried on in butter
and eggs, and butterine is manufactured here. The army
clothing factory, erected by Sir Peter Tait, who died in
18go, furnishes employment to upwards of 1,000 hands.
Other industries of Limerick comprise the manufacture
of condensed milk, flour, tobacco &c.; there are also
extensive tan vards and a distillery.

The city is lighted with gas from works purchased by
the Corporation in 187g, from the local gas company, and
the supply is now controlled by a mixed representative
committee of five mémbe“' The water works are also
the property of the Corporation.

Thpn; gﬂrgmtion of L?Lﬁeric!i existed both by preserip-
tion and charter. The city received privileges, fran-
chises and charters from the early date of 1193, when
John Stafford was made ita first provost; the frst
mayor being Adam Servant, in r1g3. The provisions in
the charter conferred by James JII. remained in force
until the Act of the 4th of George IV. (1823-4), called
the “ Limerick Regulation Act,” partially remcdelled
the powers of the Corporation. The “ Municipal Reform
Act™. became law in Limerick g Nov. 1841 The
present corporate body, at}'led the “Mayor, Aldermen
and Burgesses of Limerick,” consists of eight aldermen
and 32 councillors, elected from oub of the eight
wards into which the city is divided. The mayor holds
a court of conscience weekly, for adjudicating claims of
368, 1od. or under. The assizes for the county and
county of the city are held twice a year by the judges
of the High Court of Justice in Ireland; the court of
quarter sessions is held before the county court judge
and chairman of quarter sessions, the mayor and other
justices; petty sessions once a week, before the maycr
and some of the civic magistrates, and a police court
daily. The county court judge for the county sits four
times a year for the trial of eivil bill cases. The city
returned two members to the Irish Parliament up to
the date of the Union in 1800, and subsequently the
same number to the Imperial Parliament, until by the
% Redistribution of Seats Act, 1883," the number was
reduced to one. The parliamentary borough is, how-
ever, more extensive than the municipal.

The Town Hall, in Rutland street, contains the couneil
chamber of the City Corporation and the Mayor's,
Town Clerk’s and Treasurer's offices.

2 Ti;e Court House, near Merchants' gqonay, was erected
in 1810,

The municipal insignia include four maces, a state
sword, and a mayoral chain of very remarkable
character. The maces are similar in size, design and
workmanship: they are all 34 inches long, and their
shaits are chased with spiral foliage; each has two
knops and a graceful base knop, on which are the
names of the mavor and sheriffs for 1739; at the top
of each shaft, instead of the nsual scrolled brackets,
there are four rampant lions, facing outwards, each
resting one leg on the upper knop, and their heads
touching the heads of the maces; these are of the
usual ball cup shape, and are encireled with a garland
of foliage and flowers, partly disposed in four pendants
forming arches, under which appear the city arms—
a double-towered gateway—a tose and thistle on one
stem, 3 harp and a fleur-de-lis; above the foliage is a
narrow beaded circlet, and on the flat top are the royal
arms of George II. The sword, given by Queen Fliza-
beth in 1575, i3 44 inches long; the guard of iron-gilt is

The see of Limerick in the province of Dublin is stated
to have besn founded in the 6th century by St. Manchin,
but the bishops can only be definitely traced from 1140,
when Gillebert was appointed; in 1857 the united dio-
cese of Ardfert and Aghadoe was annexed io it. The
cathedral chuarch of St. Mary, originally built between
1142 and 1180, and added to in the 153th century, was
considerably altered in the 1;th and 15th centuries, but
still retains its cruciform plan, and consists of choir.
transepts, clerestoried nave of three bays with wide
aisles. south porch and a lofty embattled western tower
with tall sguare pinnacles at the angles, finished with
stepped battlements, the total height being 13c feet:
the tower contains & peal of 6§ bells, originally cast in
1573, but some were recast in 1829 and 38359, The
building was repaired in r739, at a cost of L1.327, when
the choir was enlarged and its arches glazed and fitting=
of classic character introduced. In 186c the cathedral
was restored, the choir new-roofed, a new east window
inserted and the floors re-laid, under the direction of
Mr. Slater, architect, of London, and in 186¢ further
repairs and alterations were effected: the total length
of the fabric is ahout 170, and the width through the
transepts go feet. The choir, continued beyond the
transepts, contains zo stalls on each side, several of
which, with their misereres, date from the 15th cen-
tury: canopies were added to the stalls at the restora-
tion of 1860: the bishop's throne, erected in 1331, from
designs by Mr. James Paine, stands at the east end of
the range of stalls on the south side, and beyond itis =
canopied seat occupied officially by the Earl of Limerick
as Prior of St. Mary's: the north transept serves as an
organ chamber, the south transept has five gradunated
lancet lights within a stepped gable, the aisles are
embattled and sunb-divided, so as to form chapels, ome
of which, on the south side, has some g stained
glass, and contains the tombs of the Earls of Limerick =
the south aisle projects beyond the transept 2nd has
some Perpendicular windows: in the south transept is a
fine monument to the Gallwey family, dated 1416, and
on the north side of the sacrarium is a large altar
tomb, with columns supporting a canopy, and recum-
bent effigies, disposed in two tiers, to Donogh O’'Briea.
4th Earl of Thomond, d. 5 Sept. 1624, and Elizabeth
(Fitzgerald), his wife, dan. of Gerald, Earl of Kildare,
d. 12 Jan. 1617: among the nuomerous other memorials
are those of Donat O'Brien, bishop 1207-17, and
Cornelius 0'Dea, bishop 1400-26. The registers, number-
ing nine volumes, date from 1726, and are kept here
under a retention order.

Opposite the cathedral is the statue of the celebrated
General Patrick Sarsfield, Earl of Lucan, who compelled
William III. to sign the treaty of Limerick; the fizure.
which stands on a high pedestal, represents him sword
in hand, leading on his troops.

The Bishop’s Palace is in Henry street, and the
grounds stretch down to O'Neill's quay, on the east
bank of the river.

St Munchin’s church, King's Island, founded in the
7th century, and rebuilt in 1827 at a cost of 1,460, is
a plain strocture, and has an embattled tower wibh
pinnacles; this church was the borial place of many
ancient Limerick families, and will seat 300 persons
Trinity church, Catherine street, was opened in 1834
and will seat 8co. BSt. John the Baptist's, in St. Johnis
square, affords 4oo sittings, and St. Michael's, in Pery

The Catholic diocese of Limerick inelndes Limerick
and a small portion of Clare. St. John's, the Catholic

is an edifice in the Gothic style of the r3th century,

entirely rebuilt on this site and remodelled in I88=
St. Joseph's church, in Quinlan street, which is attached

decorated in 1gos, is & building in the Gothic style.

The Limerick Athensum, Upper Cecil street, was
erected chiefly with the intention of developing litera-
ture, science and art, and has a lecture hall capable of

LL.D. of Skibo Castle, N.E. is now (1903) in course of

The Limerick Institution, George street, is supported
chiefly by professional men and merchants, and possesses
a reading room and a splendid library.

_ The Chamber of Commerce, in George street, formed
in the year 1808, and incorporated by roval charter in
1813, “for the promotion and protection of the trade of
Limerick,” contains news rooms and a library, s board
room for the nse of the Harbour Commissioners and
Market Truostees, and an office for the secretary and
collector of the harbour; this institution comprises

The Custom House is a fine building of hewn stone,
embellished in front with flated pilasters and capitals,

There is a theatre in Henry street.

The County Infirmary, in Mulgrave sireet, was opened

Barrington's Hn!pit&.{ at George’s quay, was founded

The City Dispensary is in Lower Gerald Griffin street,

The District Lunatic Asvlum, in Mulgrave strest, was

St. John's Fever and General Hospital is in St. John's

The Nash Home, Richmond terrace, was founded in
::39. by a bequest of Mr. John Nash, of Limerick, for

The Bank of Ireland Limited, the Provincial Bank of

There are two good hotels, and some smaller ones.

Four newspapers are published—the * Limerick

the “Limerick Leader,” M’onday, Wednesday and Fri-
da%. ‘and the “ Limerick Echo,” Tuesday and Friday.

The district around Limerick is agricultural. The
